title: MarginalizationsCommute

lean_statement.name: marginalize_marginalize
lean_statement.code:
/-- claim_3_17 fusion lemma: iterating two disjoint marginalizations
equals a single marginalization on the union,
`(G.marginalize W₁).marginalize W₂ = G.marginalize (W₁ ∪ W₂)`.
Mirrors the first half of the chained equality in the `\Lem` at
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` line 997
(`marginalizations-commute`). -/
theorem marginalize_marginalize (G : CDMG α) {W₁ W₂ : Set α}
    (h : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    (G.marginalize W₁).marginalize W₂ = G.marginalize (W₁ ∪ W₂) := by

lean_statement.name: marginalize_comm
lean_statement.code:
/-- claim_3_17 commute corollary: the order of two disjoint
marginalizations does not matter,
`(G.marginalize W₁).marginalize W₂ = (G.marginalize W₂).marginalize W₁`.
Mirrors the second half (`= (G^{\sm W₂})^{\sm W₁}`) of the chained
equality in the `\Lem` at `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex`
line 997 (`marginalizations-commute`). -/
theorem marginalize_comm (G : CDMG α) {W₁ W₂ : Set α}
    (h : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    (G.marginalize W₁).marginalize W₂
      = (G.marginalize W₂).marginalize W₁ := by

lean_explanation:
The LN states a chained equality $(G^{\sm W_1})^{\sm W_2} = (G^{\sm W_2})^{\sm W_1} = G^{\sm (W_1 \cup W_2)}$ for a CDMG $G = (J, V, E, L)$ and disjoint subsets $W_1, W_2 \subseteq V$. The Lean encoding splits this into two theorems: `marginalize_marginalize` is the fusion form (the load-bearing half — iterating two disjoint marginalizations collapses to a single marginalization on the union), and `marginalize_comm` is the commute corollary (the two orders give the same graph). Both conclude literal `Eq` of CDMGs because all sides share the carrier `α` — marginalization removes vertices but does not relabel them.

Bindings: `α : Type*` is the ambient vertex type, matching the LN's silent carrier; `G : CDMG α` is the LN's $G$; `W₁ W₂ : Set α` are the LN's $W_1, W_2$; `h : Disjoint W₁ W₂` is the LN's "disjoint" hypothesis. The LN's $W_1, W_2 \subseteq V$ precondition does not appear as a Lean hypothesis (see the design notes).

Mathlib idioms worth flagging: `Disjoint` comes from `Mathlib.Order.Disjoint` — the order-theoretic disjointness predicate, which on `Set` unfolds to $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$ and ships a built-in `Disjoint.symm`. That `.symm` is what lets the commute corollary feed `h.symm` into the swapped-order fusion call without any manual set-theoretic detour. `Set.union_comm` identifies $W_1 \cup W_2$ with $W_2 \cup W_1$ to close out the commute proof. `G.marginalize W` is the def_3_15 operation.

Shape: the fusion theorem takes a CDMG and the disjointness hypothesis and concludes one equation, `(G.marginalize W₁).marginalize W₂ = G.marginalize (W₁ ∪ W₂)`; the commute theorem takes the same data and concludes `(G.marginalize W₁).marginalize W₂ = (G.marginalize W₂).marginalize W₁`.
design_choices:
**No `W₁, W₂ ⊆ G.V` precondition — a faithful strengthening of the LN, not a divergence.** `G.marginalize W` is well-defined for every `W : Set α`, so the LN's $W_i \subseteq V$ clause is dropped from both theorems. The equality holds component-wise on $(J, V, E, L)$ for arbitrary $W_1, W_2$ because vertices in $W \setminus G.V$ are never on any walk: interior vertices of a walk of length $\geq 1$ force themselves into $G.J \cup G.V$ via `G.E_subset` / `G.L_subset`, so `π.InteriorIn W` only sees the part of $W$ inside $G.J \cup G.V$, and none of `marginalize_J / V / E / L` are affected by the "spurious" part of $W$ outside $G.V$. Without dropping the precondition the outer call in `(G.marginalize W₁).marginalize W₂` would need an extra hypothesis $W_2 \subseteq (G.\mathrm{marginalize}\,W_1).V = G.V \setminus W_1$ — but the LN proof tacitly assumes $W_2 \subseteq V$ for both marginalization steps without re-justifying it for the inner one, which is exactly the informal usage the no-precondition encoding captures. The marginalization design notes cite this row by name as one of the load-bearing iteration tests that motivated the no-precondition design in the first place.

**Disjointness `Disjoint W₁ W₂` is kept — load-bearing for both statement and proof.** The LN states "disjoint" and its walk-concatenation proof load-bears on it: a walk through $W_1 \cup W_2$ is split, via disjointness, along intermediate-vertex membership into the contribution from each marginalization step. Without disjointness the splitting is ambiguous and the inductive identification of "walks with interior in $W_1 \cup W_2$" with the two-step composition breaks. Under the fusion + commute split the same hypothesis `h` feeds both theorems: the fusion lemma uses `h` directly, and the commute corollary feeds `h.symm` into the swapped-order fusion call.

**Split the LN's chained equality into a fusion lemma plus a commute corollary, mirroring `HardInterventionsCommute.lean` (claim_3_11).** The LN bundles $(G^{\sm W_1})^{\sm W_2} = (G^{\sm W_2})^{\sm W_1} = G^{\sm (W_1 \cup W_2)}$ into one displayed equation, but the natural Lean shape is two named lemmas: a fusion rewrite rule (`marginalize_marginalize`) and a commute corollary (`marginalize_comm`). Fusion is what every downstream consumer actually rewrites with — the bifurcation-preservation argument and the d-separation-invariance theorem both `induction on #W`, peel one node at a time, and use the fusion lemma to flatten the resulting iterated marginalization back into a single $G^{\sm W}$; chapter 4+ latent-projection / hidden-variable compression, do-calculus iteration, and FCI / ICDF discovery all reach for the fusion form. The commute form is the rare-use corollary, needed only when a downstream argument has fixed which $W_i$ comes "first" and wants to reorder without collapsing. Alternatives we considered and rejected: packaging the chained equality as a single `⟨_, _⟩`-conjunction or a triple `Eq.trans` — both would force every consumer to apply `.1` / `.2` (or `.symm.trans`) at the call site, an extra projection that obscures the rewrite. Two separately-named lemmas matches Mathlib precedent: `image_image` + `Function.comp` for fusion-style; `mul_comm` / `add_comm` for commute-style.

**The third equality of the LN's triple is not a separate theorem.** $(G^{\sm W_2})^{\sm W_1} = G^{\sm (W_1 \cup W_2)}$ is exactly `marginalize_marginalize G h.symm` followed by `Set.union_comm`; any consumer that wants it can derive it in one step from the fusion lemma. Promoting it to a named theorem would just bloat the API without adding expressive power.

**Naming `marginalize_comm`.** Standard Mathlib `_comm` suffix for commutativity-of-an-operator lemmas (`add_comm`, `mul_comm`, `Set.union_comm`), pairing naturally with the `marginalize_marginalize` fusion name; mirrors `hardInterventionOn_comm` of claim_3_11.
